summary: Abraham Joshua Heschel (1907-1972) was a Polish-born American Jewish theologian, descended from Hasidic dynasties and trained in Berlin, who escaped Europe and taught at the Jewish Theological Seminary. His theology of 'radical amazement' and 'depth theology', his account of the prophets and the 'divine pathos' (the God who is moved by and involved with humanity), and his treatments of sacred time in The Sabbath and of revelation in God in Search of Man are his central contributions; he was also a prominent voice in the American civil-rights movement and in interfaith dialogue at the Second Vatican Council.
description:
Abraham Joshua Heschel, born in Warsaw in 1907 into a family descended from distinguished Hasidic dynasties, received a traditional Hasidic and rabbinic upbringing before pursuing a secular and academic education, earning a doctorate in Berlin with a study of prophetic consciousness (Die Prophetie, 1936). Expelled from Germany and narrowly escaping the Holocaust, in which members of his family were killed, he reached the United States, taught briefly at Hebrew Union College in Cincinnati, and from 1945 held a professorship at the Jewish Theological Seminary in New York. Edward K. Kaplan's two-volume biography (Prophetic Witness, with Samuel H. Dresner, and Spiritual Radical) reconstructs this arc from Hasidic Warsaw through Berlin to his American decades of scholarship and public activism.

Heschel's theology begins from wonder. He speaks of 'radical amazement,' an astonishment at being that precedes and grounds religious thought, and of a 'depth theology' concerned with the pre-conceptual acts and situations out of which belief arises rather than with belief's formulated propositions. In The Prophets (an expansion of his Berlin dissertation) he develops the idea of the 'divine pathos': for the biblical prophets, he argues, God is not the unmoved absolute of metaphysical theology but a God who is concerned, moved, and affected by what human beings do, so that the prophet is one who is attuned to and shares in this divine pathos, a 'sympathy' with the God who cares. In The Sabbath he presents the seventh day as a 'sanctuary in time,' developing a theology in which Judaism sanctifies time rather than space, calling the Sabbath 'a palace in time' and 'a cathedral' built of the hours. In God in Search of Man: A Philosophy of Judaism he sets out a fuller account of revelation, mitzvah, and the human response to a God who, in his striking reversal of the usual formula, is himself in search of the human being. These are presented here as Heschel's own attributed theology, in his own terms; Shai Held's study reads them together around the theme of transcendence, the movement from self-centredness to God-centredness.

Heschel was also a prominent public figure. He took a leading part in the American civil-rights movement, marching alongside Martin Luther King, Jr. in the 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery march and afterward writing that he felt his 'legs were praying,' and he opposed the Vietnam War. He was active in Jewish-Christian interfaith dialogue, consulting during the Second Vatican Council on the Catholic Church's statement concerning the Jews (Nostra Aetate); Kaplan documents his role, including a private audience with the Pope. His interfaith engagement is stated here factually, as his attributed activity, and is not ranked against other traditions. He is presented on Judaism's own terms as a theologian and philosopher of religion, with the biographical and scholarly literature recorded alongside as attributed sources.
editorial_notes:
This entry presents Heschel on Judaism's own terms as a theologian and philosopher of religion (neutrality #1, #4, #6). His civil-rights activism (Selma with Martin Luther King, Jr.) and his interfaith work at the Second Vatican Council (consultation on Nostra Aetate) are stated factually as his attributed activity, NOT ranked against other traditions and not framed as adjudicating between religions (neutrality #10-#11). 'Radical amazement,' 'depth theology,' the 'divine pathos,' the Sabbath as a 'sanctuary in time,' and God 'in search of man' are given as Heschel's own theology in his own vocabulary, attributed to him, not asserted as the tradition's settled doctrine.

HONESTY / status: this entry is REVIEWED-ONLY. Heschel's major works (The Prophets, 1962; The Sabbath, 1951; God in Search of Man, 1955) are twentieth-century proprietary texts under copyright, so I could NOT read a licensed open primary line-by-line for the load-bearing passages. The primary corpus is therefore cited as verified to exist and be on-topic and rated MEDIUM; the characteristic phrases ('radical amazement,' 'divine pathos,' 'a palace in time,' 'sanctuary in time,' God 'in search of man,' 'my legs were praying') are Heschel's well-attested formulations as reported across the biographical and scholarly literature, flagged as not personally verified against a licensed copy. No high rating is claimed. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-9: (a) academic biographers (Edward K. Kaplan and Samuel H. Dresner, Abraham Joshua Heschel: Prophetic Witness, Yale, 1998; Kaplan, Spiritual Radical: Abraham Joshua Heschel in America, 1940-1972, Yale, 2007, a National Jewish Book Award winner); (b) the primary corpus (Heschel, The Prophets; The Sabbath; God in Search of Man); (c) a contemporary Jewish scholar (Shai Held, Abraham Joshua Heschel: The Call of Transcendence, Indiana, 2013). No encyclopedias (Encyclopaedia Judaica, the Jewish Encyclopedia, Chabad.org, My Jewish Learning, the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources.

Relation targets: hasidism and conservative-judaism are dangling forward references; both are same-batch targets (Heschel descended from Hasidic dynasties; he taught at the Jewish Theological Seminary, the flagship institution of Conservative Judaism). tanakh exists (data/works/judaism/tanakh.json). martin-luther-king and second-vatican-council are dangling forward references carried by contemporary_of / related_to edges for his activism and interfaith work.

lifespan.note: 1907-1972 CE. Born in Warsaw into a family descended from Hasidic dynasties; doctoral study in Berlin; expelled from Germany and reached the United States, teaching at Hebrew Union College and then, from 1945, at the Jewish Theological Seminary in New York, where he remained until his death in 1972.

relations.note: Heschel articulated the concept of the 'divine pathos,' the God who is concerned, moved, and affected by human action, in The Prophets; the prophet is one attuned to this pathos. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created concept entity.

Heschel taught from 1945 at the Jewish Theological Seminary in New York, the flagship institution of Conservative Judaism; recorded as institutional affiliation. Same-batch dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created school entity.

Heschel was descended from distinguished Hasidic dynasties and raised in a Hasidic milieu, a formation that shaped his piety and theology; stated as intellectual and spiritual influence. Same-batch dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created school entity.
The Prophets and God in Search of Man rest on Heschel's reading of the Hebrew Bible, especially the prophetic books; the tanakh entry exists (data/works/judaism/tanakh.json).

Heschel took a leading part in the American civil-rights movement, marching with Martin Luther King, Jr. in the 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery march; stated factually as his attributed activity. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.

Heschel was active in Jewish-Christian interfaith dialogue and consulted during the Second Vatican Council on the statement concerning the Jews (Nostra Aetate); stated factually, not ranked against other traditions (neutrality #10-#11). Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created event entity.

sources.citation: Heschel, Abraham Joshua. The Prophets. New York: Harper & Row, 1962 (an expansion of his Berlin dissertation Die Prophetie, 1936). Cited as the primary corpus for the 'divine pathos,' the account of prophetic consciousness as sympathy with the God who is moved by and involved with humanity, in contrast to the unmoved absolute of metaphysical theology. Verified to exist and be on-topic; the characteristic formulations were NOT read line-by-line against a licensed open copy (the work is under copyright) and are reported as attested across the scholarly literature.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference. Text not reproduced beyond short attributed phrasing.

sources.citation: Heschel, Abraham Joshua. The Sabbath: Its Meaning for Modern Man. New York: Farrar, Straus and Young, 1951 (reissued by Farrar, Straus and Giroux). Cited as the primary corpus for the theology of sacred time, the Sabbath as a 'sanctuary in time' and 'a palace in time,' and Judaism's sanctification of time rather than space. Verified to exist and be on-topic; not read line-by-line against a licensed open copy (under copyright).

Heschel, Abraham Joshua. God in Search of Man: A Philosophy of Judaism. New York: Farrar, Straus and Cudahy, and Philadelphia: Jewish Publication Society, 1955. Cited as the primary corpus for 'radical amazement,' 'depth theology,' the account of revelation and mitzvah, and the reversal by which God is himself in search of the human being. Verified to exist and be on-topic; not read line-by-line against a licensed open copy (under copyright).

Kaplan, Edward K., and Samuel H. Dresner. Abraham Joshua Heschel: Prophetic Witness. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1998. The first volume of the standard scholarly biography, covering Heschel's early life in Hasidic Warsaw, his education, and his years in Europe up to his emigration; cited as the principal academic-biographical lens on his Hasidic descent and formation. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Yale University Press), not read line-by-line.

Kaplan, Edward K. Spiritual Radical: Abraham Joshua Heschel in America, 1940-1972. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2007 (recipient of the National Jewish Book Award). The second volume of the biography, covering Heschel's American decades: his teaching at the Jewish Theological Seminary, his major theological writings, his civil-rights activism with Martin Luther King, Jr., his opposition to the Vietnam War, and his role in Jewish-Christian dialogue at the Second Vatican Council; cited as the academic-biographical lens on his American career and public activism. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Yale University Press), not read line-by-line.

Held, Shai. Abraham Joshua Heschel: The Call of Transcendence. Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 2013. A contemporary Jewish scholar's systematic study reading Heschel's thought around the theme of transcendence (the movement from self-centredness to God-centredness), and treating wonder and radical amazement, theological method, revelation, and the divine pathos; cited as the contemporary-scholarly lens. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Indiana University Press; reviews in Jewish Review of Books and Project MUSE), not read line-by-line.
